Sheddie, wow, I have not thought about doing that. What benefits do one get from using it that way instead of dark Iodine? Except it not staining? Sally?

### Sheddie — 2020-04-15T18:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-288704>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=288690)
>
> Sheddie, wow, I have not thought about doing that. What benefits do one get from using it that way instead of dark Iodine? Except it not staining? Sally?

I really don't know if 'effectiveness' is significantly altered or a dose of extra Vit C is gained. I've only liked using Lugol's mixed up from granular iodine and potassium iodide. Recently, I found some of these 'lost' chemicals I'd obtained back when they were not publicly restricted due to use in illicit meth labs. I made up my last and final liter of Lugol's and figured in a small loss of strength because the chemicals were over a decade old. I had run out and was still able to get the 2% online but found it never absorbed so well, and, it stained almost as badly!

Long ago I remembered making some 'clear' Lugol's for dental use, using ascorbic acid. I can't say how much to add but what I did recently was just took aside a small amount of my fresh batch of Lugol's and added a little Vit C at a time until the volume turned almost clear... I have arthritic hands so I was always spilling and staining from my use of Lugol's! (If you act fast enough you can help a stain, say on a counter, by very quickly sprinkling ascorbic acid on it and wiping up, sprinkle again and leave for later...) I would still use the dark Lugol's to 'paint' on skin and monitor how fast it disappeared; and perhaps gauge I need more or less. If the skin patch is still there say 8+ hours later, I take that as I have sufficient in my system for the time being...

I have had poor thyroid function for over 25 years. I developed an allergy to the bovine source after some years, and all the synthetics made me much worse. Thus, ever since, I've supplemented with Lugols, a little T3, and a compounding pharmacy formula with 'dessicated thyroid,' not bovine. (Not all at the same time!) And I include trying for food sources when I have some, mostly whole fish broth and small whole fish, like sardines, etc. (Inger, I still want to try Fish Head Smoothies!!)

No, I never get 'normal' test results, always low, but not as bad as early on. If I feel especially sluggish or just can't get warm enough, I'll usually wonder about low thyroid and take up with consciously supplementing again. I don't subscribe to 'magic bullet' single pharmaceuticals except I have some rescue inhalers for my chemical sensitivities in case I run into something that triggers an asthma attack. Most natural supplements are very 'slow' to give me a sense of being helpful, or not. I'm actively supplementing from time to time with any number of natural herbals, superfoods, and such, but do not sustain long periods of the same items day after day. Not 'scientific' -- I know! I'm more interested in good clean Food, although that's difficult to believe is enough now. Best luck if you try clarifying Lugol's Iodine with Vit C. I could never determine if the added Vit C neutralized the Iodine in vivo... Maybe someone else would know? Cheers~~~

### nonchalant — 2020-04-16T01:10:35+0300

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=288690)

> Sheddie, wow, I have not thought about doing that. What benefits do one get from using it that way instead of dark Iodine? Except it not staining? Sally?

Hi Inger. Besides the staining, I've found that Lugols seems to take a long time to absorb, if I put a drop on the skin and rub in a circle. A small amount stays in a small drop on the skin for a long time. It's a small problem, really. Just spread it out more thinly.
[@Sheddie](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20386/) , I think the clarifying with vitamin c would turn much of the iodine into iodide. Which isn't necessarily a bad thing.

# Thread: Too much protein?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 86
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Haley — 2015-02-06T22:56:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-154938>

I have been doing the leptin reset for a month now and in the beginning felt I was loosing weight (at least water/inflammation). But now I feel I may be gaining slowly. I understand there are many factors (sun, climate/location, EMFs...etc.) and that the leptin reset can take some time especially in regards to weight loss. And I am absolutely not doing this protocol just for weight loss but I am trying to understand it a little better.

Since cutting carbs and increasing seafood- I realize I have increased my protein intake drastically. My estimation is I probably eat about 2lbs of seafood a day. I think I eat about 2T of coconut oil a day and then leafy greens. When I get hungry (and i have been since starting this protocol) I tend to go for more seafood aka protein. So I am wondering if this is what is keeping me from being in "ketosis" (as I know in other paleo ketogenic diets-protein must be kept at moderate levels) and reaping the benefits such as weight loss.

I seem to still be reacting to foods that are not ok for autoimmun-ers so my diet is really limited
I'm not sure how to go about increasing my fat? All I really use is coconut oil or tallow. But I'm lost on ways to get more of this in each day?

Do you think the protein is the issue? What to do about it? Suggestions or comments would be so appreciated!

### Haley — 2015-02-06T23:05:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-154942>

Im also wondering if it is the reason for my increase in hunger.

### Linz — 2015-02-07T11:10:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-154955>

Haley, you can increase the fats by adding them to your green veg. Finish cooking by adding bacon fat or perhaps olive oil and garlic, lard, duck or beef fat and heat carefully not to burn the fats. Bacon fat is great with seafood and don't use saturated fat with seafood as it blocks proper use of the seafood fats. Saturated is best in summer, more o6 in autumn and o3 in winter. Mushrooms absorb a lot of fat in cooking and you can add garlic, parsley or other herbs to them as well.

### Inger — 2015-02-07T13:12:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-154958>

I can eat fat just pure with a spoon......... i never had an issue getting enough fat, I love it!

2 T coconut oil is nothing  I think you really need to increase your fats. Your body needs them for healing!

You would be shocked at how much fat I eat  I can easily put 50-100 grams of raw grassfed butter in my morning coffee, additionally have another 50 g of butter later in the day + avocado... herring... egg yolks.. salmon.. salami rolled around raw butter.. MHS.....
The other day I had a bowl of raw salmon for dinner, about 1 pound, with 1 avocado..... and then for dessert I had 4 big egg yolk whipped up with 3 TBSP of raw butter and 1/3 teaspoon of raw honey... it was divine
I think about the fat and cholesterol as my hormone fuel. I try to get a lot of it!

I would also suggest to think more about healing and less about weight... listen to your body... what it needs. It sure will tell you
Going hungry is no good. If you have restricted your food for a long time and your body needs the nutrients, a small weight gain in the beginning could be what you need..... you will lose it again no worry!
I think we women really need to be careful to look too much about having a skinny body..... the body ideal of women today is screwed anyways
we need to want to be healthy, not skinny.
I know how hard this is when you suffer from an ED. I did too. I think maybe you will always have it harder than others.. like an alcoholic. We need to tell ourselfs that we are beautiful... with our curves and all. And then we need to do the right stuff, feed our bodies properly, get good rest, avoid stress, spend time outside, avoid EMF etc etc. Then everything will just get better and better

### Haley — 2015-02-07T20:26:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-154980>

**Linz**\-i probably eat about a couple cups of greens (chard or kale usually-ocassionally broccoli) per day so do you recommend I put several tablespoons on that or can i increase my veggies? Using online tools I estimated 2-3 cups gets me at about 15g of carbs/day. I would love to have a little more though. I have noticed many other people on a basic paleo ketogenic diet eating far more veggies than I do and maintaining ketosis. Some even have some berries, but I'm a asumming thats a big no during the winter? Im still trying to understand how the eli-paleo ketosis is different than a "basic" ketogenic diet...is it because its more seasonally focused? more DHA? What do you recommend I cook seafood in-since olive oil can't withstand high heat?

**Inger-**I LOVE butter! I wish it didn't make me retain so much water..if i could eat butter,i would lather it on everything too! Oh..same with eggs...mm..not sure if they truly give me problems but I am trying to stay AIP since some of the AIP foods give me obvious symptoms. How much avocado do you think is appropriate during winter months? I do love avocado....I think more than anything I'm feeling frustrated with my lack of choices so I end up eating more protein-which is storing as fat, giving me poor sleep, etc. I cut out the coconut butter and replaced it with more protein so I am tempted to go back to that and just monitor it better (maybe not eat it at night). And-you are absolutely right...its a slippery slope..thinking thin means healthy and fit..strong...i know i feel best when I am thinner but i know when i was too thin i felt awful as well. I would just like to be comfortable & HEALTHY of course.

### Linz — 2015-02-08T11:08:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-155024>

Haley, I don't measure food quantities or markers of ketosis. I just eat seasonally and what feels right. Bacon fat is the best for seafood. Epi-paleo is very nutrient dense with lots of DHA and sulphur, and the timing recommendations are a huge part of it. I think wanting to eat a big quantity of appropriate (energy/nutrient/dHA dense ) food at the right time is a great sign of the body trying to replace everything it lacks, and don't worry about eating what seems a huge amount of the right things (eg BAB) at these times, just be careful with sugar related cravings at other times and I don't have any experience of ed and the difference it makes.

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-02-08T16:46:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-155030>

Hi, Haley  This article from caloriesproper might be of help: [http://caloriesproper.com/dietary-protein-ketosis-and-appetite-control/](http://caloriesproper.com/dietary-protein-ketosis-and-appetite-control/)

>
> **Inger-**I LOVE butter! I wish it didn't make me retain so much water..if i could eat butter,i would lather it on everything too! Oh..same with eggs...mm..not sure if they truly give me problems but I am trying to stay AIP since some of the AIP foods give me obvious symptoms. How much avocado do you think is appropriate during winter months? I do love avocado....I think more than anything I'm feeling frustrated with my lack of choices so I end up eating more protein-which is storing as fat, giving me poor sleep, etc. I cut out the coconut butter and replaced it with more protein so I am tempted to go back to that and just monitor it better (maybe not eat it at night). And-you are absolutely right...its a slippery slope..thinking thin means healthy and fit..strong...i know i feel best when I am thinner but i know when i was too thin i felt awful as well. I would just like to be comfortable & HEALTHY of course.

Maybe you can get beef fat, if you cant tolerate butter for now? I get grassfed beef fat here very cheap.. I love to eat it raw, sliced.. it is yummy  I seem to tolerate butter very well now... I used to feel it made me swollen too but somehow it does no more? Weird!
I think it is maybe not good to eat too much avocado either... I eat.. maybe a few / week. Sometimes there are many weeks I eat none.....
Maybe you just need to test, make your own N=1 and watch what you feel better with? Try stuff out for a week or so. I am sure you will figure it out. people are so different... we just need to experiment and find what our body wants and needs. But I am sure you need more fat. I would be careful with the coconut butter tho. Maybe better eat fresh coconut, or coconut oil? I really do not do too well with coconut butter... but sure... maybe you tolerate it just fine  That is why it is hard to give advice as we are so different....
I try to let the nature guide me when I get confused.
And what always makes me feel amazing are the fish head smoothies...lol and seaweed smoothies. Any raw seafood...
You can cook the fish gently is some water and then pour olive oil over it after it is ready. Frying food is not ideal anyways. If I cook anything I try to do it very gently.. better to use moist cooking methods....
Make sure you get great quality EVOO. I think if it is not great quality, cold pressed and fresh you better leave it out......

You eat a lot of veggies in comparison to me..lol I eat practically none.. just a bit raw cucumber and onion and wild greens when I am in the woods.....

### Haley — 2015-02-09T22:34:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-155155>

**Linz-**Is bacon fat not a saturated fat? Thank you for your insight-very helpful!

**Inger**\-Thanks for your reminder to cook foods gently-often i am tempted to sear it on high heat and leave the inside rare, maybe that isn't the best option. And cooking in water is a good suggestion. I have been using more of the tallow..don't like the taste of it raw but its great for cooking veggies in. I have been eating a low amount of veggies for about a month now but I always feel better when I add a few more in. However, I realize during the winter that may not be ideal. But its been in the high 50's and sunny here all week-feel like its spring! Im curious how it is affecting all of this.

**nicld**\-Im not sure if my local health food store has duck fat or not-I haven't every seen any. Been wanting to try it though! Sounds delicious! And duck salami? mmmm...

As an update for my own biohacking this week-I decreased protein the last two days as well as so far today. I realize I was probably eating about 32oz at least of protein per day. A month ago, before the leptin reset I was eating about 16oz and thought that was a lot! So the last couple days I decreased it to about 18-20oz meat protein. So my breakfast has stayed the same in regards to protein...added half an avocado and more tallow..and this keeps me feeling much more satisfied! Then for lunch (since I'm still at least-psychologically-stuck on having it) I eat greens & fat (coconut butter, avocado, tallow). Then for dinner I'm having about 6oz protein with veggies & fat.

So overall\-I have increased my fat intake by A LOT, decreased my protein intake by A LOT and increased my veggies by a little. I've added in things like cucumber, sprouts, raw greens, mushrooms and avocados. Overall I am feeling more satisfied, less cravings and sleeping better. Also I feel that some of my inflammation has gone down. One thing I have noticed is an occasional headache the last couple days. But my speculation is that this is because my body is now for _the first time since starting the leptin reset-able to start entering into ketosis._ Before, all of that protein was being used as glucose and now my fuel is able to change to that of fat. From what I read this can take 3-4 weeks. So hopefully I can enter it in sooner-maybe being low carb already-has given me an advantage.

Im also hoping I can increase my total calories per day with this regime. I am late on my cycle and I have to wonder if it is because I have been very low carb + low calorie. And after not having a menstrual cycle for 3 years in the past..I don't want to do that to myself again!

All of you have wonderful suggestions & i am truly grateful. I realize a lot of this _has_ to be me doing my own n=1...which it has been for so long. Naturally-I turn to other humans for all the answers though, too. And this can leave me running a million directions at once. I feel freed up now to try a few things and just keep..._well, trying!_

### Haley — 2015-02-09T22:52:07+0200

### Inger — 2015-02-10T09:48:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-155190>

Haley, Jack has said when you live in high nnEMF then high protein is no good.... then you need more fat... and water!

That is how context matters....... a caveman living in the woods would be thriving on huge steaks and high protein, no problem.... but someone in a city today.... it could make really sick
We need to find our sweet spot
It is so cool we can experiment and look how we react, try out different thing.. and then we know

I've ben struggling to do keto and think I've been having too much protein and veggies and not enough fat. I've been scared of fat and with ED history I have to be careful. I have a tendancy to binge on sugar which is a scary habit when I've had breast cancer a few years ago. Unfortunately I find the restriction on the keto diet pyschologically makes me crave things I can't have.

With all the above in mind I would appreciate anyones thoughts on rough figures for a 52kg light build female for daily macronutrients? Atm I'm having something similar to below and wondered what anyone thought.

9am - breakfast of 4 eggs in Ghee and cup of GF beef bone broth with seeweed and sunflower or pumpkin seeds
1pm - between 140-180 grams of sardines in olive oil (not fresh but not in a tin - glass jars) and an endive head
4/5pm - Bone broth with 1 teaspoon of coconut oil and seaweed or some salmon if hungry.

I also snack on seeds and nuts (not sure if this is allowed!). Since I've cut my carbs I'm getting very hungry and wondered if I should add more calories - in fat I'm guessing?

Any thoughts appreciated.

### Lahelada — 2017-12-04T11:59:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-229465>

Hi Emma,
no post can explain what you need to do better than what you were advised by the doctor you consulted. If he advised you not to eat grassfed beef or bacon then do not. I suspect though that this is a mix of old and new knowledge.
From my reading you are intent on restricting quantity. IF does not restrict you in quantity only within the timeframe. The BAB might be exactly what you are missing. I could not say whether it should be protein or fat as I do not know all your history.
I suggested the above vegetables as they are relatively lo carb for your season and they contain sulfur. Vegetables is one thing you should restrict and the ones that you have should reflect the season. So you did well in restricting vegetables as you are heading for winter.

Re the sugar cravings my suggestion would be to keep a diary here/ resurrect the one you have. State your Grans history/Mums and your history and what you do on a daily basis. Especially what you do about light,sleep,light avoidance,EMF etc. It makes it so much easier for people to lend an additional eye.

Edit: one hour of sun at sunrise, grounded is a sterling way to start the day!! Well done .

### Lahelada — 2017-12-05T15:35:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-229536>

Emma . my general caveat is this .I think you have had a great consult with a great doctor if memory serves ? If that is the case do not change his advice for something you read in a post. Have you been advised what to eat? The Leptin reset is good, but you need to know is if you should make your breakfast fat or protein heavy.

Swimming in the sea will make you stark raving hungry. It is normal. Read the CT posts that mention Michael Phelps.
Make your breakfast the priority,not yoga. Have a big ass breakfast . I spell it out to take the edge off the words . See what happens to your hunger. You should be hungry every day before breakfast. Hungry or not,eat first thing in the morning. If it was now 12.45 and you were starving I hope you ate. There is way too many numbers in your plan. Have 3 spaced out meals every day for a good while then maybe think about reducing to two. While you still worry about quantities you need three meals.

Make the vagus nerve exercises your priority. It will really really help. It can solve so many problems.

Splendid that you are going to Mexico.

This is the thing Lahelada, Jack didn't mention what to eat. He said only this - never eat when dark ever again, do IF for 8/16, don't have to be keto just eat most of my carbs in the morning and to work towards 2 meals then 1. He didn't mention LR (this was in May) so when I later asked him on here whether I should be doing IF and LR because I wondered with my history if I was LR (and I'd read that you shouldn't combine the two), he just said 'I don't think you need it'. So I presumed he meant I didn't need to do LR, so I didn't do the BAB and continued with 10-6pm IF as the light was longer then trying to do 2 meals only. Then I got conflicting advice on here about whether to do the BAB and what hrs of IF to do and carbs etc and never had it confirmed by Jack. He just kept saying light light light (which I get and have been doing). But what to eat and when would be good to know. Are there guidelines for the decision over fat or protein at breakfast? Maybe I could work it out myself. Obviously I will clarify all this when I speak to him in Mexico. Unfortunately my ED history and a huge amount of stress this year leaving the UK and breaking up from my fiance has made things worse with food and I started binging again, so this is why I seek clear guidleines. I sound so nuts but I know I need it.

I will check out the vagus nerve exercises for sure.

For now I will stick to eating at 8, 12 and 4 and have 3 big meals with proteins and fats and vegetable carbs mostly in the morning and a little noon at 4. I need to widen my menu as at the moment its just sardines, salmon, GF beef, prawns occassionally, ghee, nuts, olives, avocados, bone broth, cononut oil, eggs (occasioinally goats cheese? - its so yummy here!) and green veggies.

I'm heading to the UK in 2 weeks so will eat more meat there like liver and kidneys and lamb chops as can get it easier there. The Mexico and lots of fish!

Will check out the Michael Phelps thing too. Still not sure if doing myself a favour by having a cold shower first thing before breakfast, it wakes me wonderfully but I'm wondering if its a stress to do the body and should be after my breakfast. myabe the Michael Phelps post will help me work this out.

### Emma Sabin — 2018-03-09T11:25:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-236746>

Hi ladies
I thought I would check in with you after my Mexico trip and meeting Jack - who is lovely  I've not got round to doing a proper blog yet but wanted to share what he said in my consult wth you as I checked a few things re this post..
1) Fat is what I need for breakfast. Carbs morning only and ideally IF or 8 hrs but no stress if not. Doesn't think I'm LR so no need to have BAB.
2) I have a double mutation on CBS snip so glutathione issues which is the redox potential. Waiting for 23 and me results to come through now.
4) I need to focus on light in my eyes - this is the key to all my problems - cancer, eating disorder, thyroid etc.
5) CT on my eyes - no need to do my body so much. he thinks my issue is not liver or sub cut fat but central leptin resistance, so I'm off to read the Leptin blog Part 1 as most relevant apparently.

There was some stuff in there also about family and breaking away from them and listening to my intuition which fits so well with my current life so that was good, but that was the main thrux of our chat.

He said mine is simple - light light light! Said I'm stronger than I think too  Yay!

If you have any thoughts regarding the above I'd love to hear. In particular I'm interested to know what you thought of having BP tea or just tea with coconut oil in? Also if supplements break the IF in the morning? And also hot water with lemon - does that break it too? I ask because I don't always have my breakfast as soon as waking as do some yoga and meditation and just wondered if a BP tea would be allowed in my IF window?

Hope you don't mind me asking

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=229536)
>
> Emma . my general caveat is this .I think you have had a great consult with a great doctor if memory serves ? If that is the case do not change his advice for something you read in a post. Have you been advised what to eat? The Leptin reset is good, but you need to know is if you should make your breakfast fat or protein heavy.
>
> Swimming in the sea will make you stark raving hungry. It is normal. Read the CT posts that mention Michael Phelps.
> Make your breakfast the priority,not yoga. Have a big ass breakfast . I spell it out to take the edge off the words . See what happens to your hunger. You should be hungry every day before breakfast. Hungry or not,eat first thing in the morning. If it was now 12.45 and you were starving I hope you ate. There is way too many numbers in your plan. Have 3 spaced out meals every day for a good while then maybe think about reducing to two. While you still worry about quantities you need three meals.
>
> Make the vagus nerve exercises your priority. It will really really help. It can solve so many problems.
>
> Splendid that you are going to Mexico.

### Jan Christer — 2018-03-10T00:47:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-236798>

> [Haley said:](/goto/post?id=155155)
>
> ...
> As an update for my own biohacking this week-I decreased protein the last two days as well as so far today. I realize I was probably eating about 32oz at least of protein per day. A month ago, before the leptin reset I was eating about 16oz and thought that was a lot! So the last couple days I decreased it to about 18-20oz meat protein.
> ...

It can be risky to eat too much protein, since it generates a lot of metabolic waste products.
The biggest risk is if a person has a problem with their kidneys.
Prof. Neil Mann says in the article below that "you can possibly go up to three or four grams per kilogram body weight without any real harm".
This can be important to read about the possible risks:
[https://thenewdaily.com.au/life/wellbeing/2017/08/15/perth-mum-protein-poisoning/](https://thenewdaily.com.au/life/wellbeing/2017/08/15/perth-mum-protein-poisoning/)

### Jan Christer — 2018-03-10T21:11:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-236866>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=236860)
>
> The facebook post also covers "macros" A pick&mix of doctors is generally not a good idea . Just saying..

I agree, we should be based on science.
Here's a quote from the paper below: "A suggested maximum protein intake based on bodily needs, weight control evidence, and avoiding protein toxicity would be approximately of 25% of energy requirements at approximately 2 to 2.5 g x kg(-1) x d(-1), corresponding to 176 g protein per day for an 80 kg individual on a 12,000kJ/d diet."

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16779921](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16779921)

### Lahelada — 2018-03-10T21:20:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-236870>

> [Jan Christer said:](/goto/post?id=236866)
>
> I agree, we should be based on science.
> Here's a quote from the paper below: "A suggested maximum protein intake based on bodily needs, weight control evidence, and avoiding protein toxicity would be approximately of 25% of energy requirements at approximately 2 to 2.5 g x kg(-1) x d(-1), corresponding to 176 g protein per day for an 80 kg individual on a 12,000kJ/d diet."
> [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16779921](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16779921)

I agree ,a relevant paper. It is an observation without context,though. If I were to look I would probably find a contrary one. Unless you can also find why one Dr recommended one thing and not the other and apply it to your personal context then it is better to follow protocol as is. You are obviously free to change protocols but protocol mixing requires a lot of data. You might be nixing the intended effect of all protocols in the mix by not seeing the synergies between the recommendations.

### Jan Christer — 2018-03-10T21:45:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-236877>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=236870)
>
> I agree ,a relevant paper. It is an observation without context,though. If I were to look I would probably find a contrary one. Unless you can also find why one Dr recommended one thing and not the other and apply it to your personal context then it is better to follow protocol as is. You are obviously free to change protocols but protocol mixing requires a lot of data. You might be nixing the intended effect of all protocols in the mix by not seeing the synergies between the recommendations.

Right, the paper is just based on general research, but it's an established fact that excessive protein intake in general becomes toxic, so it's important to be aware of that.
But also, like you say, particular protocols can also deviate from general guidelines.

You can access the full article here, I thought you might find it interesting:

### dennis.london@gmail.com — 2018-04-26T17:47:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-protein.12842/post-239973>

Getting caught up on the posts and this one seems to jump out at me simply because I was always hungry in the beginning which had me wanting to snack as well. For me, having the BAB (Big Ass Breakfast) was the key to removing the snacks - which in turn pushed my Leptin reset into high gear. Starting my day with 3 eggs, 3 or 4 slices of bacon, and half an avocado was what held me, not just up to lunch, but well into the afternoon - sometimes until dinner. That typically relied on my daily activity level, of which I didn't (and still don't) get a ton of due to my job sitting at a computer all day. Once the snacking demon was removed I was well on my way to optimal. Just remember - it won't happen overnight and you will have to quiet that demon with something. What worked for me was drinking a big glass (12 to 16 oz) of water whenever I felt the need to snack. I believe Dr Kruse mentioned somewhere that our snacking urge could be tied to improper hydration...especially if you're in a high EMF area.

I know it seems fairly basic, but honestly, this is what worked best for me.

Emma - by no means did I ever go to just one meal a day. Sorry if it came across that way. What I meant to convey was that by eating a BAB I was able to basically eliminate the snacking urges while getting my Leptin reset. I would still have a small lunch, typically something like a childs portion or a cobb salad (or whatever). Then dinner would be a nice steak or preferably some seafood. Of course we would try to eat earlier as you don't want to eat 4 hours prior to going to bed...so, if you typically go to bed at 10pm you'll want to be done eating by 6pm.

# Thread: MRI With Contrast
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-with-contrast.12843/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2015-02-07T05:48:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-with-contrast.12843/post-154949>

Maybe only Jack would have an opinion on this issue, but does anyone have data on how much less effective an MRI would be at finding:

\* pituitary tumor
\* aneurysm

if done without contrast?

Would the use of a high resolution 3 Tesla MRI machine change that answer?

My doctor wants me to get an MRI with contrast, and I am concerned about the gadolinium contrasts that are used in these tests. There are lawsuits against the companies that make those contrasts, and there are peer reviewed studies showing non trivial percentages of people who get those contrast studies later develop kidney problems. The chelate forms of the contrast supposedly are safe, but there is some evidence that the toxic gadolinium gets absorbed directly anyway.

The bottom line: is the MRI without contrast only 30% as useful? 90% as useful? I'm trying to understand the tradeoffs here.

### billy — 2016-03-12T02:52:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-with-contrast.12843/post-190689>

I need to know the answer to this as well.
:bump:

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-12T03:29:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-with-contrast.12843/post-190695>

MRI without contrast is not ideal for the tumor. It is useless for an aneurysm.........MRA is better option for CV lesion

### billy — 2016-03-12T06:35:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-with-contrast.12843/post-190701>

Thanks! ....and dammit - the sides look nasty.

Then there's this 2015 Radiology published [study](http://pubs.rsna.org/doi/abs/10.1148/radiol.15150025);

"Transmission electron microscopy showed that most of the gadolinium deposits were in the endothelial walls. However, 18% to 42% of the gadolinium appeared to have crossed the blood-brain barrier and was deposited in the neural interstitium.

'Certainly, that wasn't expected; we didn't think these agents could do that,' said Dr McDonald."
….

"These authors have tried to exclude known causes of blood-brain barrier breakdown, leading to the clear suggestion that this retention of gadolinium in the endothelium and brain parenchyma is not due to local effects of tumor or inflammation."
\- Dr Nuwer

[http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/842107#vp\_2](http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/842107#vp_2)

This action allows binding and polarization to separate water into subatomic particles that are positively and negatively charged. This action is called building or expanding the exclusion zone (EZ) of water. Dr. Gerald Pollack's experiments showed these effects. Dr. Gilbert Ling proved by experiment that each molecule of ATP in a cell controls 8,800 water molecule binding sites and 20 potassium ions to allow water to become structured inside every cell of your body.

The first step in photosynthesis and in mitochondrial oxidation/phosphorylation of electrons is to charge separate water...........now you know the why K+ and sunlight help muscle pain and fatigue reverse. ### Sandra — 2018-01-11T06:18:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-with-contrast.12843/post-232221>

I opted to have an MRI instead of a mammogram to avoid the radiation. When I arrived for the appointment today I was told there would be a contrast dye used. It surprised me a little as I'd not been aware it would be required and didn't have any time to look into it. I agreed as the radiographer said it was fine (of course!). Now I am annoyed at myself as I have found out that the gadolinium is a heavy metal and I just want to get it out of my system. I don't have any renal issues and have always had fine kidney function. Since the appointment I've been drinking loads of reverse osmosis water (probably silly to think that will flush it out if it's already absorbed somewhere!). I on a magnetico mattress. I usually have lots of sun in my life too. Is there anything more I can be doing to remove this quickly? Thanks

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-11T14:35:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-with-contrast.12843/post-232229>

Dialysis.........but i would not do that. You should be fine. Just limit your MRI imaging in short periods of time.

# Thread: More damaging news on statins for Big Pharma
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-damaging-news-on-statins-for-big-pharma.12849/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-09T01:59:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-damaging-news-on-statins-for-big-pharma.12849/post-155086>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25655639](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25655639)

Let's see ... statins disrupt mitochondrial function via depletion of CcQ10 and heme A, which, in turn, depletes ATP. They also inhibit K2, which protects arteries from calcification, as well as selenium, which is both neuro and heart protective. Yet we have people saying they're so "effective" and "harmless" that they should be added to the water supply ([http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22779765](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22779765)). Statins meet at the intersection of both bad science and bad pharma

### Lahelada — 2015-02-09T02:58:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-damaging-news-on-statins-for-big-pharma.12849/post-155092>

Beggars belief. I gave the South Beach diet the heave ho when Dr A started selling ready meals and espousing the virtue of said statins.
Life that needs a pill to generically sustain itself has something quite fundamentally wrong with it.

### Inger — 2015-02-09T21:44:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-help-my-friend.12863/post-155147>

I need some help.

I have a friend... she cant log in here, she has tried many times. I promised to ask for her instead.

So she cannot gain weight

She is a few years older than me, a little longer.. and weights only 37 kg

She has tried everything but she cant gain. She was able to gain a little bit some times ago when she tried hard core keto and ate a LOT of meat and fat etc. but she said she could not keep it up as she had to eat so big amounts and she felt just sick in the end. She thinks she might not be able to do ketogenic..... like Jeremy? but she cant tolerate carbs very well either... because of blood sugar swings.. it really is difficult. \--------------------------------------
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amenorrhoea#Diagnosing\_Secondary\_Amenorrhea](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amenorrhoea#Diagnosing_Secondary_Amenorrhea)
**Secondary amenorrhoea** (menstruation cycles ceasing) is often caused by hormonal disturbances from the [hypothalamus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypothalamus) and the [pituitary gland](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pituitary_gland), from premature [menopause](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Menopause) or intrauterine scar formation.
\-------------------------------------------

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amenorrhoea#Diagnosing\_Secondary\_Amenorrhea](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amenorrhoea#Diagnosing_Secondary_Amenorrhea)

Secondary amenorrhea's most common and most easily diagnosable causes are pregnancy, thyroid disease, and hyperprolactinemia. A pregnancy test is a common first step for diagnosis.[\[23\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amenorrhoea#cite_note-23) Hyperprolactinemia characterized by high levels of the hormone prolactin, is often associated with a pituitary tumor. A dopamine agonist can often help relieve symptoms. The subsiding of the causal syndrome is usually enough to restore menses after a few months.

Secondary amenorrhea may also be caused by outflow tract obstruction, often related to [Asherman's Syndrome](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asherman%27s_Syndrome).

**Asherman's syndrome** (AS) or **Fritsch syndrome**, is a condition characterized by adhesions and/or fibrosis of the endometrium most often associated with [dilation and curettage](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dilation_and_curettage) of the intrauterine cavity

\=============================================

Re-check H-Pylori
Check for parasites
Blood test for:
prolactin
TSH, T4, FT4, T3, FT3, RT3
IGF-1, IGFBP3

check for uterine fibroid obstructing menstrual outflow?
**
Have she had this done??
**Dilation**(or dilatation) and curettage** (D&C) refers to the [dilation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cervical_dilation) (widening/opening) of the [cervix](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cervix) and surgical removal of part of the lining of the [uterus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uterus) and/or contents of the uterus by scraping and scooping ([curettage](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curettage)).

......

if fibroids -------->> iodine deficiency??

### dantothep — 2015-02-10T17:25:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-4-stumped-atp-beta-ox-questions.12880/post-155237>

Apologies if I have missed something key here....hopefully someone can help. This is proper head melting stuff.

JK talks about beta-oxidation being more efficient at creating ATP, this I get and read up a little bit elsewhere also.

Questions:

1\. How long does beta oxidation take to create 147 ATP from one molecule of an 18 carbon stearic acid?

EMF 4 alludes to it happening in sleep...but I thought i read that any extra energy (ATP) from the day is burned off as heat at night?
​

2\. If ATP can't be stored, what happens to the extra ATP made if it isn't used at that time?

3\. How does beta-oxidation help short intense exercise such as weightlifting, jumping etc?

Is the take home for the non biochemist/medical geek, just to adapt to eating more fat as your energy source? And not carb loading post workout, as the fat should sustain your energy levels longer so you don't instantly need the quick carb hit?

### Jonathin — 2015-02-10T19:41:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-4-stumped-atp-beta-ox-questions.12880/post-155261>

I think the most important point is that ATP allows protein unfolding so that water can form charged exclusion zones of water. That is how energy manifests. The question is to what degree your body can accomplish and maintain that charged hydration. ATP is a cog in the machine not the source of energy. Too much carbs breaks down mitos especially at Complex 1 and DNA damage. Kinda like killing the goose that laied the golden egg except the effect is slower and less obvious at first.

### Mihaly — 2015-02-10T19:43:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-4-stumped-atp-beta-ox-questions.12880/post-155263>

Im also looking forward to read the answers 1-2.
3\. I think we have enough readily avaible ATP pool for 1-2 second (jumping, heavy lifting), and after that creatin-phosphate system is working on regenerating the ATP (10-15 second). even in ketosis. In longer exercises (above 10 second) B-oxidation starting to dominate the ATP regeneration.
If you want to be better fat adapted, than you shouldnt do carb loading pre and post workout. it can take 1-2 months (or even more if you were "carboholic") to switch from carb depending sport metabolism to fat and ketone dominating.

### Mihaly — 2015-02-10T19:51:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-4-stumped-atp-beta-ox-questions.12880/post-155264>

I think if you are more and more fat adapted than your anaerobic treshold is going up, you burn less and less glucose in higher intensities and you can comfortably move without lactic acid buildup. So you shouldnt avoid anaerobic training. Its ok to do it during the transition, but your anaerobic performance will suffer in the first few weeks. When you are fully keto-adapted, your muscles doesnt burn glucose, just ketones and fatty acids even in very high intensity

### dantothep — 2015-02-10T20:42:04+0200

>

> 3\. I think we have enough readily avaible ATP pool for 1-2 second (jumping, heavy lifting), and after that creatin-phosphate system is working on regenerating the ATP (10-15 second). even in ketosis. In longer exercises (above 10 second) B-oxidation starting to dominate the ATP regeneration.
> If you want to be better fat adapted, than you shouldnt do carb loading pre and post workout. it can take 1-2 months (or even more if you were "carboholic") to switch from carb depending sport metabolism to fat and ketone dominating.

But this paragraph:

Cross fitters live in this pathway constantly because of their WOD are done for **time alone**. The ATP-CP system has a special problem built in for fast ATP recyclers. The methylation of guanidinoacetate to form creatine **consumes more methyl groups than all other methylation reactions combined in the human body**. This implies the more you use the ATP-PC system the more likely you will become a major slow methylator whether you have the 677 or 1298 SNP. This is why vegans, performance and endurance athletes fall apart early, have horrible [vitamin D](http://www.jackkruse.com/the-sunshine-of-your-life/) levels, major dopamine/serotonin issue, trouble with their ammonia cycles, and have horrible endogenous glutathione recycling.

### Penny — 2015-02-11T21:03:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-4-stumped-atp-beta-ox-questions.12880/post-155382>

**Nicotinamide riboside, a trace nutrient in foods, is a vitamin B3 with effects on energy metabolism and neuroprotection.**
[Chi Y](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Chi%20Y%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24071780)1, [Sauve AA](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Sauve%20AA%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24071780).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24071780#)**

**Abstract**
**PURPOSE OF REVIEW:**
This review focuses upon the biology and metabolism of a trace component in foods called nicotinamide riboside. Nicotinamide riboside is a precursor of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD), and is a source of Vitamin B3. Evidence indicates that nicotinamide riboside has unique properties as a Vitamin B3. We review knowledge of the metabolism of this substance, as well as recent work suggesting novel health benefits that might be associated with nicotinamide riboside taken in larger quantities than is found naturally in foods.

**RECENT FINDINGS:**
Recent work investigating the effects of nicotinamide riboside in yeast and mammals established that it is metabolized by at least two types of metabolic pathways. The first of these is degradative and produces nicotinamide. The second pathway involves kinases called nicotinamide riboside kinases (Nrk1 and Nrk2, in humans). The likely involvement of the kinase pathway is implicated in the unique effects of nicotinamide riboside in raising tissue NAD concentrations in rodents and for potent effects in eliciting insulin sensitivity, mitochondrial biogenesis, and enhancement of sirtuin functions. Additional studies with nicotinamide riboside in models of Alzheimer's disease indicate bioavailability to brain and protective effects, likely by stimulation of brain NAD synthesis.

**SUMMARY:**
Initial studies have clarified the potential for a lesser-known Vitamin B3 called nicotinamide riboside that is available in selected foods, and possibly available to humans by supplements. It has properties that are insulin sensitizing, enhancing to exercise, resisting to negative effects of high-fat diet, and neuroprotecting.

# Thread: CT Variables, Shivering, Warming up
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-variables-shivering-warming-up.12892/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### dantothep — 2015-02-11T18:54:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-variables-shivering-warming-up.12892/post-155377>

I'm writing this post in the hope that it gets stickied, after spending ridiculous amounts of time trying to find what should be a simple answer. _I will remove the above txt if it does. Caveat I may not have all of these correct so please jump in and correct me._

Variables for people experiencing a hard transition into the cold tub (based on about 30 different threads):

_Variable = Target_

1.  Length of stay = 45mins (JK's original variable)
2.  Temperature of Water = 50-55° f (JK's original variable)\*
3.  Ice packs on chest = Building up to 30lbs of ice.
4.  Ambient Temp of room = ideally not heated, but may need to be heated in the first instance.
5.  Depth of submersion = Ideally whole body except hands, feet & head.
6.  Compression Vest = 75% of the time not included in Rx or easy start guide for the bath.
7.  Hat, Gloves & Scarf = Initially then removing.
8.  Daily Frequency = 2 x 30 mins better than 1 x 60 mins.

_Gauges_

1.  Shivering in bath = should be mild (i,e, you should be able to voluntary stop them for a few minutes) if it is violent or you have complete loss of motor control, get out.
2.  Length of time shivering on exit = more than 1 hour is probably not ideal.
3.  Frostbite/Urticaria = Not good.

Warming up should ideally be done at ambient temps.

\*@Shijin13 mentioned that colder water temp reduced warming times?

Other things to consider/test/ask Jack if still having a hard time

1.  T3 levels
2.  ???

# Thread: How does the marine food chain link to land food chain?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-the-marine-food-chain-link-to-land-food-chain.12894/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-12T03:24:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-the-marine-food-chain-link-to-land-food-chain.12894/post-155419>

Where is the base of the Epi-paleo Rx?
PUFA are involved in the regulation of physiological processes by serving as precursors in the biosynthesis of bioactive molecules, and both PUFA and sterols are essential to major membrane constituents of crustacean zooplankton, zoobenthos, and fish. According to Brett & Müller-Navarra, all herbivores convert the short-chain PUFA (i.e., with <20 carbons, DHA has 22) α-linolenic acid (18:3ω3) to long-chain PUFA (i.e., with ≥20 carbons), such as EPA and DHA, albeit with different efficiency. In aquatic environments, most PUFA and sterols originate in the primary producers, essentially the photosynthetic autotrophs. Bacteria are generally poor in PUFA (DHA), especially long-chained PUFA (DHA). EPA-producing bacteria are not common in marine environments, yet Yazawa et al. found 88 strains of bacteria capable of producing EPA out of 5000 strains screened. In fact, it was recently found that several marine bacteria contain EPA and DHA at levels as high as 25% of total cell membrane FA , and it is likely that the polyketide synthase (PKS) pathway for PUFA synthesis (that acts independently of FA elongase and desaturase activities to synthesize EPA directly) is widespread in marine bacteria. This is the key in the marine food chain and goes all the way back 580 million years ago (Cambrian explosion) to when O2 showed up in the oceans after the sun increased its UV light by 10% and this stepped up DHA production from photosynthetic marine organisms where it was first was synthesized on Earth.
Additionally, the culture conditions of the oceans may determine the bacterial EPA content that is dependent on pH, temperature, and other growth conditions. This includes the blue light and nnEMF around it because water's hydrogen bonds REACT to electromagnetic fields all the TIME. We forget this. Some crustacean species (Epi-paleo Rx alert) and nematodes may directly feed on bacteria and act as the first consumers of primary DHA production, forming an important link between the basis of marine food webs and the higher trophic levels, like large metazoans and fish.
Direct feeding on bacteria may be crucial for crustacean species living close to hydrothermal vents, high-pressure low-temperature deep-sea (Seafood and cold linked) habitats and permanently cold marine environments, or when bacteria is the sole item available for food. It is worthy to note that in both deep-sea habitats and in the anoxic sediment of intertidal flats, bacteria may produce PUFA. Nevertheless, the ecological role played by bacteria in aquatic environments cannot be dissociated from that of heterotrophic protists (HP).
These Protista are also known as Protozoa, which means “first animals”; in fact, the term does not mean that all protozoa are the ancestors of the animals, but refers to the fact that they ingest food, which is typical of animals and not plants. They are able to disconnect from the sun and land to survive.
Some of these Protista are predators and feed on bacteria and other Protista, i.e. they feed on dead organic matter, while others are parasites that exploit the resources of several organisms.
They can be divided into three main groups depending on how they move:
1\. Amoeboid protozoa
2\. Sporozoa
3\. Flagellate protozoa
4\. Ciliates: they are considered the most developed group because of their complexity. They can live either in freshwater or salty water
Heterotrophy is practiced by “mixotrophic” protists that include flagellated phytoplankton that may ingest bacteria or other protists and by heterotrophic protists that do not possess permanent chloroplasts (photosynthesis) and rely on other organisms for nutrition. HP's are important consumers of bacteria and phytoplankton in oceanic food webs concentrating DHA, these, in turn, act as regenerators of nutrients for further phytoplankton growth and as a food resource for marine zooplankton for fish consumption. Although HP has been described as early as the 1920's, their ecological importance was only fully appreciated when new analytical methods enabled their identification and quantification (e.g., epifluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry).

The notion of a classical linear food chain in aquatic environments consisting of phytoplankton, zooplankton, and fish predominated until the 1970's, and its replacement by the concept of a trophic web only gained ground in the 1980's. This is an example of a linked coupled cycle that life uses.

In the trophic web concept, dissolved (DOM) and particulate organic matter (POM) consisting of detritus, heterotrophic bacteria, and autotrophic phytoplankton are consumed by HP in a carbon-transfer pathway called the microbial food web. The key point is how atoms of carbon cycle from sea to land in this web. Humans feasted off this part of the carbon cycle. This microbial food web connects to the classical food chain that branches into a network of trophic links. Although picoplankton is responsible for the bulk of primary production in large parts of the marine environment (as well as lakes), their small size makes them largely unavailable for direct consumption by crustacean zooplankton, which has difficulty in retaining these size particles in their filtering apparatus. In systems where microalgal species-rich in high-quality lipids (_i.e._, DHA and other key PUFA's) dominate the phytoplankton and can be directly grazed by crustacean zooplankton (Epi-paleo Rx), the trophic transfer from autotrophs to crustaceans via HP can be considered both as a loss of carbon (_i.e._, losses via respiration), as well as a loss of essential lipid compounds.
Autotrophic Protists: are mostly algae. The term algae embrace all photosynthetic protists. It refers to an aquatic, photosynthetic way of life, not an evolutionary kinship. Most algae live in water, but some are terrestrial. Most algae live near the surface of the water producing **30 to 50 percent of the earth's oxygen**. _This is where the oxygen for DHA construction began._ Algae are classified on the basis of conservative characteristics such as the type of cell wall, flagella, photosynthetic pigments, and the form in which food is stored.

Nevertheless, HP feeding on nanophytoplankton and bacteria may biochemically enhance the quality of the tissues of their prey, which led to the “trophic-upgrading” concept developed by Breteler _et al._ This is how DHA was concentrated in the marine food chain. Thus, heterotrophic protists bridge the gap between the microbial loop in the oceans and higher trophic levels by both repackaging their food and by increasing its nutritional value, which may be especially important when phytoplankton abundance is low or of reduced lipid quality and producers are dominated by prokaryotic picoplankton. All these things are eaten or filtered by crustacean and bivalves and wind up in fish.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-12T04:07:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-the-marine-food-chain-link-to-land-food-chain.12894/post-155423>

This helps explain why the DC current is linked to regeneration in all plants and animals on this planet. Dr. Robert O. Becker's research found on the surface of water and proteins there is a brisk DC current. All tissues need a higher redox potential to regenerate. A low redox means low DC current and low tissue DHA levels.
**Pay attention to sunsets and dim light at night.** Though light was known to influence the circadian mechanism via melanopsin, according to the literature, its effects on non-circadian processes were considered minor from 2005 to 2013.

That is now changing, especially when you understand how dim light really works in the brain. It has been recently shown in mammals that when melanopsin signaling is altered in the CNS for any reason at all, **mammals lose the ability to catch up on sleep permanently**. As they lose the ability to sleep, and they simultaneously lose autographic efficiency and _their regenerative potential_ for all tissues, and **they lose the ability to tap into Becker’s DC current.**

When you lose the DC current, it means your redox potential is bad. It also means your tissues are deficient in DHA. **This is why a lack of sleep often leads to disease and an early death**. Autophagy is absent in these cases. Over time, these organisms will lose their memory and they will gain more fat mass as this alteration of melanopsin continues.

### seanb4 — 2015-02-18T14:35:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-155960>

To expand on this and maybe give further insight, all my symptoms are far worse after eating.

So maybe cortisol starts off good (taken before breakfast), thanks to sleep, but then all gets used up on the internal stresses my body faces upon eating. This would explain why my noon cortisol (low) is the opposite of morning which was high.

DHEA would then be low thanks to the chronic internal stress of digestion. The poor digestion increases stress, and stress makes poor digestion worse.

I have been taking 200mg pregnenolone over the last 5 days. So far headaches, sore throat, and palps. I assume it is causing dehydration somehow when rebalancing the adrenal hormones. Would be useful finding my T3. I plan on staying at 200mg for a few more days and see if it balances out and removes dehydration symptoms, if not reduce to 50mg. Will hold off on DHEA for now.

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:02:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156007>

(**a**) Phylogentic tree of the melastatin subfamily of TRP channels. Note that TRPM3 is the closest relative of the founding member melastatin (TRPM1). (**b**) Pregnenolone is synthesized from cholesterol and is considered to be the prohormone of all steroid hormones. 17

\-hydroxylase activity converts pregnenolone to 17-hydroxypregnenolone (170H-pregnenolone) and converts progesterone to 170H-progesterone. DHEA (dehydroepiandrosterone) is converted to testosterone and oestrogenes. Sulphonation of pregnenolone leads to the formation of PS, a robust TRPM3 agonist. (**c**) Some of the positive effects attributed to pregnenolone, explaining its epithet 'fountain of youth'.

> **A TRP channel-steroid marriage**
> Bernd Nilius[1](http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/full/ncb1208-1383.html#a1) & Thomas Voets[1](http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/full/ncb1208-1383.html#a1)
>
>
> 1.  Bernd Nilius and Thomas Voets are in the Department of Molecular Cell Biology, Laboratory of Ion Channel Research, KU Leuven, Campus Gasthuisberg, Herestraat 49, bus 802, B-3000 Leuven, Belgium.
>     e-mail: [bernd.nilius@med.kuleuven.be](mailto:bernd.nilius@med.kuleuven.be)
>
>
> **Abstract**
> A surprising functional association between TRPM3, a mysterious member of the family of transient receptor potential (TRP) cation channels, and the sulphated version of pregnenolone, 'mother' of all steroid hormones, has been identified.
>
> The TRP family ([Fig. 1a](http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/full/ncb1208-1383.html#f1)) carries the stigma of being 'promiscuous', as its members can be tempted to gate in response to a surprising variety of stimuli, including temperature and mechanical stress, as well as various endogenous and exogenous chemicals.

[http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/fig\_tab/ncb1208-1383\_F1.html](http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/fig_tab/ncb1208-1383_F1.html)

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:09:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156009>

> Abstract Steroid hormones are made from cholesterol, primarily derived from lipoproteins that enter cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. In endo-lysosomes, cholesterol is released from cholesterol esters by lysosomal acid lipase (LAL; disordered in Wolman disease) and exported via Niemann-Pick type C (NPC) proteins (disordered in NPC disease). These diseases are characterized by accumulated cholesterol and cholesterol esters in most cell types. Mechanisms for trans-cytoplasmic cholesterol transport, membrane insertion, and retrieval from membranes are less clear. Cholesterol esters and “free” cholesterol are enzymatically interconverted in lipid droplets. Cholesterol transport to the cholesterolpoor outer mitochondrial membrane (OMM) appears to involve cholesterol transport proteins. Cytochrome P450scc (CYP11A1) then initiates steroidogenesis by converting cholesterol to pregnenolone on the inner mitochondrial membrane (IMM). Acute steroidogenic responses are regulated by cholesterol delivery from OMM to IMM, triggered by the steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR). Chronic steroidogenic capacity is determined by CYP11A1 gene transcription. StAR mutations cause congenital lipoid adrenal hyperplasia, with absent steroidogenesis, potentially lethal salt loss, and 46,XY sex reversal. StAR mutations initially destroy most, but not all steroidogenesis; low levels of StARindependent steroidogenesis are lost later due to cellular damage, explaining the clinical fi ndings. Rare P450scc mutations cause a similar syndrome. This review addresses these early steps in steroid biosynthesis.—Miller, W. L., and H. S. Bose. Early steps in steroidogenesis: intracellular cholesterol traffi cking. J. Lipid Res. 2011. 52: 2111–2135.

[http://www.jlr.org/content/52/12/2111.full.pdf+html](http://www.jlr.org/content/52/12/2111.full.pdf+html)

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:14:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156012>

Fig. 4.
(_A_) CYP11A1–Adx interface. CYP11A1–Adx contacts involve the interaction domain (F helix) and loop surrounding the Adx \[2Fe-2S\] cluster (yellow) and occur on the proximal surface of CYP11A1 (green). Direct hydrogen bonds between residues Lys109CYP11_A_1-Ala45Adx,, Trp418CYP11_A_1-Leu80Adx, and water-mediated hydrogen bonds, including residues Met120CYP11_A_1-Thr49Adx and Met120CYP11_A_1-Ala51Adx are displayed. Dashed lines indicate salt bridges and H-bonding interactions; water molecules are represented as small red spheres. (_B_) Predicted electron-transfer pathway from the \[2Fe-2S\] cluster to the heme iron proceeds from Fe1 of the \[2Fe-2S\] cluster via side- and main-chain atoms of Cys52Adx and Ala51Adx, through-space jump (3 Å), Gln422CYP11_A_1, Cys423CYP11_A_1 to the heme iron.

[http://www.pnas.org/content/108/25/10139/F4.expansion.html](http://www.pnas.org/content/108/25/10139/F4.expansion.html)

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:22:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156014>

> In humans, the precursor to all steroid hormones, pregnenolone, is synthesized from cholesterol by an enzyme complex comprising adrenodoxin reductase (AdR), adrenodoxin (Adx), and a cytochrome P450 (P450scc or CYP11A1). This complex not only plays a key role in steroidogenesis, but also has long been a model to study electron transfer, multistep catalysis, and C–C bond cleavage performed by monooxygenases. Detailed mechanistic understanding of these processes has been hindered by a lack of structural information. Here we present the crystal structure of the complex of human Adx and CYP11A1—the first of a complex between a eukaryotic CYP and its redox partner. The structures with substrate and a series of reaction intermediates allow us to define the mechanism underlying sequential hydroxylations of the cholesterol and suggest the mechanism of C–C bond cleavage. In the complex the \[2Fe-2S\] cluster of Adx is positioned 17.4 Å away from the heme iron of CYP11A1. This structure suggests that after an initial protein–protein association driven by electrostatic forces, the complex adopts an optimized geometry between the redox centers. Conservation of the interaction interface suggests that this mechanism is common for all mitochondrial P450s

[http://www.pnas.org/content/108/25/10139.full.pdf+html](http://www.pnas.org/content/108/25/10139.full.pdf+html)

### JanSz — 2015-02-24T00:28:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156423>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=156042)
>
> Yeah this is kinda what I based my dosage on, I know **Jansz started with zero pregnenolone** so I though I probably have more so will start with less.

I had barely detectable pregnenolone
undetectable progesterone
low cortisol throughout the day, but it was fluctuating in about the right way,
double digit DHEAs, about 90 when desirable is 490.
\============

But you appear to have plenty of cortisol on 24hr basis.
You are missing proper distribution of cortisol over 24hr.
So, unless you test and prove otherwise, it may be useful to assume that you have acceptable pregnenolone and progesterone.

I would stay away from pregnenolone supplementation, at this time.
But you said that DHEA is low, then supplement with DHEA pills.

///

### seanb4 — 2015-02-24T10:25:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156455>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=156423)

> undetectable progesterone

> \============
>

> You are missing proper distribution of cortisol over 24hr.
> So, unless you test and prove otherwise, it may be useful to assume that you have acceptable pregnenolone and progesterone.
>
> I would stay away from pregnenolone supplementation, at this time.
> But you said that DHEA is low, then supplement with DHEA pills.

This is interesting, thanks jansz.

I might drop down from 200mg to 50mg preg for a while then. My impression was that if dhea was low then upstream I must be low in other hormones / preg and that taking preg isn't harmful...

Also since my body goes from high morning cortisol straight to low noon cortisol doesn't this suggest my body needs extra cortisol after breakfast to deal with the extra stress of eating and thus the right amount of cortisol for me is higher than reference range?

Anyway recently I have been having poor sleep, bed 8pm, get up 8am, wide awake for about 4hrs middle of the night.
This has happened before when I wasn't on hormone supps and I increased my afternoon meal size.
This time I have also increased my meal size so it could be my body going to sleep real early to sleep off the stress of the meal and this then messes up my circadian cycle later in the night as I wasn't meant to be tired that early.
OR it is the hormone supps. Last night I tried taking 50mg preg at afternoon to see if it would help me keep awake until a reasonable time but it did not.

### seanb4 — 2015-02-24T10:31:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156458>

Also I have been supping DHEA 50mg for a few days now. Noticed erections have improved.

### Ake Bono — 2015-02-25T19:47:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156594>

any other changes when taking dhea? good or bad?

"Been tested for intolerance's which come up fine." - mine also. I am totally "clean" no intolerances but still Im "intolerant" to some food . histamine or sulphur...
"Recently my calprotectin level came up high." - mine also. but took me many tests to find infections. btw h.pilory (if this is your problem) came after several negative endoscopies...stool sample.

"My stomach expands/is heavy, my heart rate raises massively, particularly strong pulsation in my abdomen, I feel nauseous/sickly full. Belching occurs. I feel weak and tired." - have the same reactions but at least for me is clear h.pilory and amonia/histamine release.

"have you tried colloidal silver?" - not yet. cant find a proper one in my country? would you mind sharing the source you use? thanks

### seanb4 — 2015-02-26T10:10:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156687>

> [Ake Bono said:](/goto/post?id=156594)
>
> any other changes when taking dhea? good or bad?

Yeah, I think it might be the DHEA that's disrupting my sleep, not had a good nights since I have been taking it, however I have also noticed my digestion improve slightly, less heaviness, feel like I can eat more, and I'm a lot warmer at night so it is helping my metabolism. I am going to keep my current dosage of 50mg preg + 50mg DHEA (morning) for another few days and see if my sleep stabilizes. Else I will either half the DHEA dose or drop the pregnenolone.

> [Ake Bono said:](/goto/post?id=156594)
>
> "Been tested for intolerance's which come up fine." - mine also. I am totally "clean" no intolerances but still Im "intolerant" to some food . histamine or sulphur...
> "Recently my calprotectin level came up high." - mine also. but took me many tests to find infections. btw h.pilory (if this is your problem) came after several negative endoscopies...stool sample.
>
> "My stomach expands/is heavy, my heart rate raises massively, particularly strong pulsation in my abdomen, I feel nauseous/sickly full. Belching occurs. I feel weak and tired." - have the same reactions but at least for me is clear h.pilory and amonia/histamine release.

Very interesting. How did you find out about your amonia/histamine intolerance? Is it just by eating histamine foods or did you do some kind of test. I can't really say I've noticed a difference with histamine foods but I haven't thoroughly tested the possibility, I just seem to react to everything.

H.Pylori would make so much sense for my symptoms but after the negative tests and all the various advanced and specific protocols and supplements to combat it I have noticed no diference. I do not think it is this that is causing my problems but maybe a similar infection? Hopefully I will find out when my stool tests comeback.

> [Ake Bono said:](/goto/post?id=156594)
>
> "have you tried colloidal silver?" - not yet. cant find a proper one in my country? would you mind sharing the source you use? thanks

I think it might help you, you seem to have similar symptoms as me and it made a noticeable improvement for me. In fact Jack recommended it as soon as I posted about my calprotectin levels. I assume it would help by suppressing the bugs in the gut (hpylori in your case) which allows the immune system to work better which means less leaky gut, better liver function, and less overall inflammation.

Anyway I used silver botics ([http://www.healthjunction.co.uk/ame...er-32-fl-oz-946-ml?filter\_name=silver biotics](http://www.healthjunction.co.uk/american-biotech-labs-silver-biotics-10-ppm-silver-32-fl-oz-946-ml?filter_name=silver%20biotics)) but it is expensive as balls so it maybe cheaper to make your own especially if you can't import.

I'm using a DIY one right now but I'm not sure on the quality TBH, asin maybe it is producing too large particles that could cause blue skin. I guess it's okay short term anyway until I learn to make it right.

Anyway you need two 12-14 guage 99.95+% silver wire. 3 9V batteries linked together and connected to the silver wires via ordinary wires/crocodile clips. You need to hang the 2 wires into a litre/half litre jar filled with distilled (important) water. Leave the live wires suspended in the water for 10hrs ish and you've got colliodal silver. It will have a metalic taste if done right so keep testing. Should come to less than £20.

### seanb4 — 2015-03-04T10:28:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-157397>

Spent 8 days on micronized Pregnenolone and DHEA at 50mg each. Noticed digestion improved as did overall energy however sleep was still poor. Not as poor as it was the first few days however I was waking up regularly through the night. Always felt tired come morning. Overall I felt better on preg 50 + dhea 50 than I did without any supps but I feel I can tweak this so sleep isn't destroyed.

Going to take jansz advice and drop the preg for a week starting today and note the changes. Also the new brand of DHEA I just got isn't micronized so I am changing 2 variables at once. I'm taking 400mg of this as apparently only 10-15% gets absorbed properly orally. Would this work out roughly the same dose as the micronized or is there some other reason I can't use non-micronized?

### seanb4 — 2015-03-06T14:52:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-157631>

On day 3 of taking just DHEA without Preg. Sleep seems better however digestion seems worse. I am getting heaviness in my stomach again and my stomach feels "closed off" more and I can't drink as much water even though I am thirsty.
It's still early days but I would take the benefits in digestion over the benefits of sleep...
I will wait at least a week on just DHEA and if digestion is still bad add pregnenolone in smaller doses. Maybe 25mg.

### seanb4 — 2016-01-21T21:50:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-187028>

Hey @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/), hope you don't mind looking over this old thread again. I'm considering trying the DHEA again. I stopped it ages ago as it was causing severe palpitations and breathlessness, also ruining my sleep. Do you think the dosage was too high? What should I do differently?
